data.title: Fund Manager Roundup: U.S. Treasurys in Focus Amid Rising Debt
data.description:
The performance of U.S. Treasurys is a key discussion topic among asset managers as the nation's debt is expected to continue rising. While Europe is stepping up defense spending, investors' concerns about fading U.S. exceptionalism are expected to keep European assets in demand. The following is a selection of asset managers' comments.

Anticipated Soft Data Expected to Support Short End of U.S. Treasury Curve

1217 GMT - The front end of the U.S. Treasury yield curve might be supported by softer economic activity numbers over the summer, RBC BlueBay Asset Management's Mark Dowding says in a note. Meanwhile, the long end could be troubled by concerns about fiscal irresponsibility and the rising trajectory of U.S. debt, he says. "Officials in the administration have noted that they want to skew issuance toward shorter-dated debt at a time when they think yields are 'too high' and this may be technically supportive for longer-dated bonds," Dowding says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Short-Duration Linkers Have Performed Well Lately

1136 GMT - Short-duration inflation-linked bonds have been one of the standout asset classes in recent years, in terms of risk-adjusted returns, AXA Investment Managers Investment Institute's Chris Iggo says in a note. "The beauty of short-duration bonds is they benefit from the inflation accrual, linked to a reference consumer price index, but have limited sensitivity to interest rate volatility," he says. The total return, year-to-date, for the ICE Global one-to-five year Inflation Linked Bond index, was 7.5% in U.S. dollar terms as at the end of June, he says. In euro terms, with the dollar exchange rate hedged, the return was 2.95%, still well above the increase in the eurozone HICP, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

U.S. Treasury Yield Curve Expected to Steepen

0932 GMT - RBC BlueBay Asset Management is confident that the shape of the Treasury yield curve will steepen, investment chief Mark Dowding says in a note. When a bond yield curve steepens, the gap between short- and long-dated bond yields widens. "Prospects for the next Fed Chair being elected as a dove to deliver rate cuts may benefit the front end of the curve," Dowding says. "But [it] will be a worry with respect to longer-dated maturities, given the potential threat to price stability and a weakening of the Fed's inflation mandate," he says. BlueBay, meanwhile, continues to express no clear directional view on U.S. Treasury yields. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Treasury and Dollar Performances Signal Approaching End of U.S. Exceptionalism

0919 GMT - The golden era of U.S. economic and geopolitical leadership--or U.S. exceptionalism--is coming to an end, Pictet Asset Management says. "We are seeing the first signs of this with the breakdown in the relationship between the dollar and U.S. Treasurys," Pictet says. Historically, yields on U.S. Treasurys have moved in the opposite direction to the currency, it says. "Whenever turbulence hits, investors worldwide would gravitate to the perceived safety of U.S. Treasurys and the dollar, sending yields lower and the greenback higher." However, since President Trump's so-called Liberation Day tariff announcements, that link has broken, with yields having risen while the dollar has weakened. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

U.S. Tax Cuts Could Put Long-Term Strain on Capital Markets

0849 GMT - President Trump's major budget package, which was passed by Congress, could push U.S. debt higher, J.P. Morgan Asset Management's Tilmann Galler says in a note. "If the tax cuts remain in place permanently, this could cause U.S. debt to rise to over 130% of GDP," the capital market strategist says. While the program could generate positive growth effects in the short term, it could also lead to significant disruptions in bond markets, he says. "For example, confidence in the U.S. dollar is beginning to dwindle as a consequence." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Candriam Sticks to Long Duration in Eurozone Bonds, Neutral on U.S. Treasurys

1331 GMT - Candriam maintains a long duration position in the eurozone and remains neutral on U.S. Treasurys, Nadege Dufosse says in a note. In a diversified portfolio, duration continues to offer effective protection, particularly in a slowing economic environment with moderate inflation, the global head of multi-asset says. The European Central Bank appears to be close to the end of its easing cycle, although two further rate cuts are still anticipated by year-end, she says. "This dynamic, combined with a still sluggish macroeconomic backdrop and well-anchored inflation, supports our constructive view on European sovereign debt," Dufosse says. In the U.S., Candriam awaits a more attractive entry point in Treasurys, looking for a slight decline in yields in the second half of 2025. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Structural Headwinds Seen for Long-Dated Government Bond Yields

1006 GMT - Long-dated government bond yields face structural headwinds, Edmond de Rothschild Asset Management's Benjamin Melman says in a note. "Long-term rates in the U.S. are at risk owing to the government's intention to continue lowering taxes without truly offsetting the revenue loss with public spending cuts," the global chief investment officer says. In the rest of the world, public investment needs are increasing substantially due to the geopolitical shift, he says. Term premiums--the additional yield investors demand to hold a long-dated bond rather than a short-dated one--are rising and should continue to grow, Melman says. "Overall, we prefer to steer clear of long duration bonds, as we feel the risk of a recession is moderate," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

U.S.'s Ability to Repay Debt Isn't at Risk

0932 GMT - The U.S.'s ability to repay its debt remains intact, UBS Global Wealth Management says in a note. It expects the U.S. house to approve President Trump's economic policy agenda, although the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office estimates the bill will add $800 billion more to the national debt than the initial House version over the coming decade, UBS GWM says. "But we think the U.S. remains able to manage its debt," UBS GWM says. The Federal Reserve's credibility, the U.S. dollar's reserve status, deep and liquid Treasury markets, Fed balance sheet holdings, and bank capital regulations could all help finance the deficit, it says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Long-Dated U.S. Treasury Supply Expected to Stay Under Control

0841 GMT - Supply of longer-term U.S. Treasurys should stay under control, UBS Global Wealth Management says in a note. A key investor concern behind the sharp rise in the long end of the U.S. Treasury curve earlier this year was the prospect of a ballooning supply of long-dated Treasury bonds needed to finance the growing federal debt, it says. However, recent auctions have shown a stabilization in investor sentiment, it says. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent earlier this week also indicated that a ramp-up in sales of longer-dated securities is unlikely given where yields are now, UBS GWM says. "Instead, we expect more short-term Treasuries, such as T-bills, will be issued until the Federal Reserve resumes policy easing later this year," UBS GWM says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

High Yields Appeal But Rising U.S. Debt Levels Are a Concern

1340 GMT - Schroders sticks to a neutral view on government bonds, its multi-asset investment team says in a note. High yields might be appealing but they risk moving higher, and Schroders is particularly concerned about rising debt levels in the U.S. "Yields are higher and valuations look better, but we still have concerns about rising debt and ongoing inflation risks in the U.S.," Schroders says. Outside of the U.S., inflation is less of a worry, it says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

U.S. Treasury Yields Expected Lower in The Summer

1254 GMT - Generali Asset Management expects Treasury yields will fall over the summer due to a weak economy as well as heavy bond supply, it says in a note. This should bring 10-year Treasury yields to the lower end of a 3.75%-4.75% range, it says. The 10-year Treasury yield is last up 4 basis points at 4.289%. Generali AM keeps to small duration positions within bonds, implying a low exposure to bonds that are sensitive to interest-rate changes. Supply of German Bunds is also set to be high, Generali AM says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Debt Sustainability Is a Major Concern

1000 GMT - Debt sustainability is a major concern and deserves serious debate, both in the U.S. and elsewhere, Degroof Petercam Asset Management's Sam Vereecke says in a note. The most prudent path is fiscal discipline, the CIO for fixed income says. Current yields make bonds appealing, although many investors remain cautious and are wary that yields could rise, he says. The key uncertainty for investors is gauging what the peak levels in yields will be and the point at which it is worth buying the bonds again, he says.(emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Longer Bond Duration in Europe Has More Appeal Than in U.S.

1430 GMT - Bonds with longer durations have more appeal in Europe, as well as parts of Asia, than they do in the U.S., says BlackRock's Rick Rieder in a note. "In a market environment marked by heightened uncertainty and unreliable correlations, the case for a larger allocation to stable, income-generating investments has never been stronger," the CIO of global fixed income says. "Lending opportunities in Europe and parts of Asia offer compelling ways to gain longer duration exposure." However, this isn't the case with U.S. Treasurys. BlackRock favors short- and medium-dated Treasurys due to concerns that inflation risks could prevent interest-rate cuts from the Federal Reserve. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Bond Investors Can Get High Income From Short-Dated Bonds

1417 GMT - Investors should prioritize income over duration, says BlackRock's Rick Rieder. Yields across the board have rarely been this high, especially at the short end of the curve, the CIO for global fixed income says in a note. This gives investors the chance to earn very attractive returns from high-quality borrowers with little duration risk. "With the Federal Reserve likely to keep rates above neutral for longer, debt markets are offering historically high income levels to investors once again," he says. "Even with inflation running higher than it has historically, these nominal yields dramatically eclipse run-rate inflation, something rarely seen over the prior few decades," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

European Equities Look More Attractive Than U.S. Equities

1123 GMT - European stocks have more favorable relative values than U.S. stocks, Robeco's Jan Mosselaar and Dijana Kostic say in a note. "The region continues to trade at a discount relative to the U.S. market, which many investors see as a compelling entry point," they say. U.S. valuations could, nonetheless, be justified due to stronger emphasis on shareholder value in the U.S., versus a more stakeholder-oriented focus often seen in Europe, the portfolio managers say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0837 ET - Credit risk premium on euro-denominated credit remains relatively low despite uncertainty around trade policies ahead and the end of a pause on so-called U.S. reciprocal tariffs on July 9, LBBW credit analysts say in a note. The credit spreads, or risk premium, has dropped from the high levels that followed the U.S. announcement on global tariffs on April 2. A general appetite for risk has contributed to the decline in euro credit spreads, the analysts say. The iTraxx Europe Main index, which tracks euro investment-grade credit default swaps, trades at 54 basis points below the 63bps posted before the U.S. announced wide-ranging global tariffs on April 2, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0817 ET - The front end of the U.S. Treasury yield curve might be supported by softer economic activity numbers over the summer, RBC BlueBay Asset Management's Mark Dowding says in a note. Meanwhile, the long end could be troubled by concerns about fiscal irresponsibility and the rising trajectory of U.S. debt, he says. "Officials in the administration have noted that they want to skew issuance toward shorter-dated debt at a time when they think yields are 'too high' and this may be technically supportive for longer-dated bonds," Dowding says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0815 ET - The most supportive outcome for the dollar in the near-term would be if the U.S. pursues targeted tariffs, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. The July 9 deadline for the reimposition of higher reciprocal tariffs after a 90-day pause is nearing. The U.S. could impose tariffs on some countries that haven't made significant progress on trade deals while extending its reprieve for those making headway, the analysts say. "Targeting individual countries in a trade war tends to strengthen the dollar, while broad-based tariffs generally weaken it." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0813 ET - The dollar's reaction if the U.S. postpones its reciprocal tariffs again is uncertain, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. A 90-day pause in the tariffs is due to end on July 9. A further pause might be interpreted as a reluctance to implement the tariffs, boosting risk sensitive currencies, they say. The dollar could get some initial support as the worst-case scenario for the U.S. economy if the reimposition of high tariffs is averted, they say. However, fewer tariffs could prompt the market to price in further U.S. interest rate cuts. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell recently indicated he would have cut rates already if it weren't for tariffs lifting inflation forecasts. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0811 ET - Covered bonds by Belgian banks appear more attractive than senior preferred bonds by the same banks, ING's Maureen Schuller says in a note. Belgian preferred senior bond provides only around 14 basis points of pickup versus covered bonds, making the preferred senior bonds less appealing, Schuller says. "This is 13bps less than at the start of this year and the smallest spread differential between the two of the past five years." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0802 ET - The dollar could weaken in the worst-case scenario regarding the potential reimposition of U.S. reciprocal tariffs that are currently paused, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. A 90-day pause in the tariffs is due to end on July 9. The most-severe outcome would be the U.S. imposing the previously announced higher tariffs on all trading partners without a trade deal, the analysts say. The dollar could sell off against the euro, Swiss franc, yen, and the pound. Smaller, less-liquid currencies such as emerging market currencies would also fall, they add. The DXY dollar index last trades down 0.2% at 96.966. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Gold futures rise, shrugging off losses earlier in the session on stronger-than-expected U.S. Nonfarm Payroll data. Futures are up 0.1% at $3,347.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal is on track to finish the week flat after recouping losses from Thursday's jobs report, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. The report was fairly mixed overall, but the slight decline in the unemployment rate should make the Federal Reserve optimistic that uncertainty around U.S. tariff policy hasn't yet hit companies severely, Commerzbank says. The Fed will likely continue to hold off cutting interest rates, a headwind for non-interest bearing bullion, analysts write. Still, gold's main support is from volatile U.S. policy above all, which is eroding investor confidence in U.S. assets and boosting gold's safe-haven demand, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0749 ET - The Swiss franc's recent strength reflects demand for safe-haven assets ahead of the July 9 deadline when the pause on U.S. reciprocal tariffs ends, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. Even if the franc extends its strength on the day of the deadline, gains will be limited, they say. The euro is unlikely fall below 0.9250 francs for an extended period, they say. The euro trades flat at 0.9344 francs. It reached a one-month low of 0.9304 francs on Tuesday, according to LSEG. The dollar hit a nearly 14-year low of 0.7869 francs on the same day. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0736 ET - Short-duration inflation-linked bonds have been one of the standout asset classes in recent years, in terms of risk-adjusted returns, AXA Investment Managers Investment Institute's Chris Iggo says in a note. "The beauty of short-duration bonds is they benefit from the inflation accrual, linked to a reference consumer price index, but have limited sensitivity to interest rate volatility," he says. The total return, year-to-date, for the ICE Global one-to-five year Inflation Linked Bond index, was 7.5% in U.S. dollar terms as at the end of June, he says. In euro terms, with the dollar exchange rate hedged, the return was 2.95%, still well above the increase in the eurozone HICP, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0725 ET - U.K. fiscal risks will remain front of mind for sterling traders for some time but the currency is unlikely to suffer any sustained losses, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. The U.K. government will be "well aware of the situation and act accordingly." Prime Minister Keir Starmer initially failed to guarantee Treasury chief Rachel Reeves would keep her job in parliament Wednesday. However, he quickly tried to contain the damage by later saying she would remain in her post for a long time. Hence, any sterling declines below $1.3600 could be a good entry point to establish long positions, or bets on it rising, the analysts say. Sterling trades flat at $1.3653. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0715 ET - The Norwegian krone could recover from the recent losses it sustained since Norway's central bank surprised with an interest rate cut last month, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. The krone's depreciation is unlikely to persist as markets could scale back rate cut bets, while Norway is better-insulated from U.S. tariff threats than its peers. UBS favors selling the euro if it rises above 12.1200 krone over the next month. The euro last trades up 0.3% at 11.8630 krone. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0657 ET - Copper prices remain elevated on China demand, supply concerns and a weaker U.S. dollar, sitting up nearly 5% on month. Base metals across the board gained over June on emerging signs of stronger Mainland China demand, alongside trade talk progress ahead of the July 9 tariff deadline and a weaker dollar, BMI analysts say in a note. Tariff threats have been a particular focal point for copper, keeping U.S. commodities exchange Comex premiums significantly above LME prices, BMI says. Traders have directed flows of the metal to the U.S. in an effort to beat a potential import tariff, squeezing supply outside the country. LME three month copper is currently down 0.8% at $9,870 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum inches 0.4% lower at $2,595.50 a ton. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Euro Credit Risk Premium Remain Low Ahead of U.S. Tariffs Deadline — Market Talk
data.description: Credit risk premium on euro-denominated credit remains relatively low despite uncertainty around trade policies ahead and the end of a pause on so-called U.S. reciprocal tariffs on July 9, LBBW credit analysts say in a note. The credit spreads, or risk premium, has dropped from the high levels that followed the U.S. announcement on global tariffs on April 2. A general appetite for risk has contributed to the decline in euro credit spreads, the analysts say. The iTraxx Europe Main index, which tracks euro investment-grade credit default swaps, trades at 54 basis points below the 63bps posted before the U.S. announced wide-ranging global tariffs on April 2, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Online Casinos in New Zealand - New Casino Sites Ranked by iGaming.com
data.description:
Auckland, 04.07, (Globe Newswire) - New Zealand is approaching a landmark moment in its online gambling history. With full regulation on the horizon and the first licensed operators expected to launch in 2026, Kiwi players will soon have access to safer platforms backed by local oversight and consumer protections.

To guide players through this transition, iGaming.com has released its 2025 guide to the best online casinos in New Zealand. Trusted since 2011, iGaming.com helps players find secure and reputable sites in an evolving gambling landscape.

VIEW THE BEST ONLINE CASINOS IN NEW ZEALAND

iGaming.com is an independent platform dedicated to providing accurate, up-to-date information on all aspects of online gambling. While originally focused on markets such as the UK, Germany, and other parts of Europe, the platform has expanded to New Zealand in response to the country’s evolving online casino landscape and growing demand for trusted, localized guidance.

The following outlines how iGaming.com evaluates online casinos in New Zealand, focusing on factors like security, licensing, player protection, and reputation, to deliver reliable and unbiased recommendations.

What Makes iGaming.com a Trusted Source

Kiwis can trust iGaming.com because the platform is built on a foundation of transparency and accuracy. In a crowded and often overwhelming online gambling space, iGaming.com stands out by delivering clear, unbiased information about online casinos that helps players make informed choices.

With New Zealand on the path to full online gambling regulation, iGaming.com has become a trusted guide for those seeking safe and legal ways to play. Meanwhile, as the government prepares to introduce a formal licensing system, the platform provides players with timely insights, practical advice, and clear rankings based on unbiased criteria.

Helping Kiwis Navigate the Changes Ahead

New Zealand’s current online gambling environment is largely shaped by offshore operators. These are accessible to players, but beyond the scope of domestic oversight. While these sites are legal to use, the lack of a local licensing framework has created a fragmented space with uneven standards for safety. That landscape is set to change.

Beginning in February 2026, the government will auction just 15 licenses, each valid for three years and subject to strict renewal criteria. Licensed operators will be required to implement robust age verification, anti-money laundering protocols, and responsible gambling tools.

As the country transitions to a regulated system, iGaming.com helps players understand how these changes affect their choices. Central to the platform is a multi-layered ranking system to evaluate gambling operators in New Zealand.

Although iGaming.com earns referral commissions, these have no influence on how casinos are ranked or reviewed. Here are the main criteria:

  • Regulatory Alignment: With New Zealand preparing to launch a formal licensing system, iGaming.com closely monitors which operators meet the criteria expected under the upcoming framework. This includes assessing compliance with age restrictions, data protection standards, and anti-money laundering protocols – key areas that will soon be mandatory for local licensing.
  • Player Protection: iGaming.com prioritizes platforms that actively promote responsible gambling. Operators are assessed for tools that allow players to manage their gaming habits, including deposit caps, cooling-off periods, and self-exclusion options. Sites must also offer clear access to support services that align with New Zealand’s emerging consumer protection policies.
  • Game Integrity: Each recommended online casino is checked for game fairness and transparency. iGaming.com favors platforms that feature games from certified developers that use independently tested random number generators. Clear RTP (Return to Player) rates are also integral to helping players understand the odds and make informed choices.
  • Payment Systems: Reliable transactions are a key part of the review process. iGaming.com evaluates payment methods based on speed, security, and trust. Casino operators are expected to demonstrate consistency in processing deposits and withdrawals and provide secure, encrypted systems for handling player funds.
  • Bonus Clarity: Bonuses are reviewed for fairness, not flash. iGaming.com focuses on the real value of each bonus package by examining wagering requirements, withdrawal limits, validity, and other restrictions that could affect New Zealand players. Only platforms with clear, reasonable conditions receive positive ratings.
  • User Experience: Ease of use is assessed across both desktop and mobile platforms. iGaming.com evaluates how smoothly a casino runs on different devices, including responsiveness, page load times, and overall navigation. The review process considers how easy it is for players to find and launch games, access their accounts, adjust settings, contact support, and manage deposits or withdrawals.
  • Customer Support Standards: Support services are evaluated for both availability and quality. iGaming.com looks for 24/7 assistance, quick response times via live chat, and helpful, well-organized FAQ sections. The goal is to make sure Kiwi players can get timely answers to common issues, from account problems to payment questions.

THE BEST ONLINE CASINOS FOR KIWIS – RANKED & RATED

Supporting Players Beyond Rankings

Though iGaming.com is known for providing expert online casino rankings, the platform also plays a key role in educating players. With changing laws and new licensing standards on the horizon in New Zealand, iGaming.com understands how important it is for players to have access to clear, trustworthy information to navigate these changes.

iGaming.com consequently provides a wide range of practical resources to help players stay informed and in control, including:

  • Guides about gambling laws and regulatory frameworks
  • How-to articles on choosing safe and regulated platforms
  • Comparative breakdowns of the best online casinos
  • Educational content on responsible gambling practices
  • Links to local and international gambling support services
  • Expert analysis of industry trends and other developments

Moreover, iGaming.com launched iGamingCare in 2024, a platform dedicated to promoting responsible gambling and player welfare. Through this program, the platform offers practical tools for managing gambling habits, including a two-minute self-assessment designed to help users spot potential risks.

iGamingCare also features regular podcasts with industry experts, mental health professionals, and responsible gambling advocates, offering insights on player protection, regulatory developments, and strategies for maintaining healthy gambling habits.

Conclusion: Why iGaming.com Stands Out

New Zealand’s online gambling space is entering a new chapter, with regulation set to reshape how and where players engage with online casinos. Against the backdrop of change, iGaming.com stands out for its player-first approach and commitment to transparency.

Rather than promoting unvetted operators, iGaming.com aims to direct players toward reputable platforms that align with emerging legal standards. This way, players can make smarter choices, avoid unnecessary risks, and stay on the right side of upcoming regulations.

Here is how iGaming.com earns player trust:

  • Independent and unbiased: Rankings are based on strict criteria that focus on quality and player safety.
  • Expert-driven reviews: Evaluations are overseen by gambling experts and compliance professionals for accuracy and transparency.
  • Regularly Updated: Listings are reviewed monthly to reflect changes in licensing, performance, and real player feedback.
  • Player-first focus: Rankings focus on what players really want and expect from a gambling platform.

VIEW THE 2025 LIST OF LICENSED ONLINE CASINOS IN NZ

About iGaming.com

iGaming.com is a trusted online resource for New Zealand players seeking clear, accurate information about online gambling. As the country prepares to regulate the industry, the platform offers expert-led casino rankings, up-to-date legal insights, and practical guides focused on player safety.

Through its responsible gambling hub, iGamingCare, users can access tools like self-assessment quizzes, support links, and educational content designed to promote responsible play. It also features podcasts with international experts offering practical advice on staying in control while gambling.

Affiliate Disclosure & Disclaimer:

iGaming.com is a free, independent comparison platform supported by affiliate partnerships. Commissions may be earned if users sign up or deposit via links on the site. However, all rankings and reviews are based on expert analysis and strict evaluation standards to guarantee transparent, player-focused content.

All operators featured for the New Zealand market are expected to meet licensing requirements set by trusted international regulators. As New Zealand moves toward formal online gambling regulation, iGaming.com will continue to monitor developments closely.

Gambling carries financial risk and can be addictive. Please gamble responsibly and only bet what you can afford to lose. For support, contact the Gambling Helpline New Zealand at 0800 654 655 or visit gamblinghelpline.co.nz.

Contact

For press inquiries or further information our PR team is happy to assist you:

data.title: Two Polish central bankers see further rate cuts this year, media reports say
data.description:
Two Polish central bankers said they expect further interest rate cuts this year, according to media reports on Friday, after the bank unexpectedly cut its main rate to 5% this week, citing expectations of declining inflation in the coming months.

The National Bank of Poland's Ludwik Kotecki said he would "personally" see room for two 25-basis-point cuts this year, if data confirms slowing inflation and a moderation in growth in central Europe's largest economy, Bloomberg reported on Friday.

A second rate-setter, Henryk Wnorowski, told Polish financial news agency PAP Biznes that he also expected further rate cuts and said he supports them being made in 25-bps increments.

He added that remaining uncertainty meant that there were several scenarios for the rest of the year that he could not rule out.

"I have said that at the end of 2025 I see the reference rate 'with a four at the front'. So one more adjustment of 25 basis points will be enough, and this condition will be met," he said.

"If there were several such adjustments, there will still be a 'four at the front'. However, I do not see a large number of moves," he said.

Central bank Governor Adam Glapinski said on Thursday that July's 25-basis-point cut in interest rates was not the start of a policy-easing cycle, though he did not rule out another such move in September.

data.title: Fitch Changes Global Power Synergy's Outlook to Stable on Moderating Leverage
data.description:
Fitch Ratings has moved Global Power Synergy's outlook to stable from negative, according to a Friday release.

The rating agency maintained the company's long-term foreign currency issuer default rating and national long-term rating at BBB- and A+(tha), respectively.

The outlook shift stems from a forecast of moderating leverage in the medium term due to a reduced capital expenditure pace, Fitch said.

The Thailand-based power producer also looks to sell a portion of its share in high-growth Indian joint venture Avaada Energy Private, which should reduce the strain on its financial profile, the rating agency said.

A robust business profile being the country's main private power producer and offshore diversification anchor the ratings, Fitch said.

The rating agency sees the company's EBITDA net leverage hitting about 6x in 2025 before gradually tempering to 5.7x by end-2028.

The decline should be supported by a solid cash flow, a pause in its investment in the Energy Recovery Unit project, and slower investments in Avaada's solar projects.

Notable changes in the company's EBITDA net leverage, business profile, or ties with parent PTT could lead to future rating actions, Fitch said.

data.title: Teck Resources Maintains Hold Rating, US$45 Price Target at Deutsche Bank
data.description:
Teck Resources (TECK-B.TO) maintained its Hold rating and US$45 price target at Deutsche Bank Research, according to the bank's second-quarter preview of the company published Wednesday.

The bank expects Teck to report an EBITDA of C$766 million in the second quarter, down from C$927 million in the first quarter and below the Bloomberg consensus of C$825 million.

The expected decline reflects operational setbacks, lower sale volumes at the QB and Red Dog mines, and higher transportation costs at QB, according to Deutsche Bank.

Deutsche Bank also expects Teck to report a small negative free cash flow in the second quarter.

"Teck has underperformed year to date (flat vs peers +15-30% in USD terms) and we see value at current levels, but delivery at QB will remain key," Deutsche Bank said. "As has been the case for some time, the prize remains a re-rating towards the established copper pure plays, if Teck can consistently achieve guidance and successfully ramp QB to nameplate capacity."

Teck's share price closed 2.1% lower on Thursday to US$40.82 in NYSE. The company will report its second-quarter results on July 24.

data.title: Multibillion-dollar HODL: Bitcoin whales awaken after 14 years
data.description:
Bitcoin whales have reawakened after 14 years of patiently holding the world’s first cryptocurrency, transferring billions of dollars worth of the world’s largest cryptocurrency.

A Bitcoin wallet “12tLs” transferred 10,000 Bitcoin worth over $1 billion after 14 years of holding the assets on Thursday.

The mysterious whale initially received the 10,000 BTC on April 3, 2011, when Bitcoin traded for around $0.78 a coin, according to blockchain data shared by Lookonchain in a July 4 X post.

Hours later, two other whale addresses — “bc1qm” and “1GcCK” — also awoke after 14 years of dormancy, each transferring 10,000 BTC, BitinfoCharts data shows.

The whales have held their BTC for 14 years, a more than 13 million percent increase, TradingView data shows.

Some cryptocurrency traders monitor whale transaction patterns as a gauge for institutional Bitcoin demand and short-term price action due to the potentially market-moving amount of capital transferred.

The transfers follow a recent trend of long-term holders cashing in. Around two weeks ago, another savvy investor realized a nearly $30 million profit after holding their Bitcoin stash since 2013 and realizing a 496-fold return on their $60,000 initial investment, Cointelegraph reported on June 23.

Public companies continue Bitcoin accumulation, as BTC rides on S&P 500 all-time high

While some early investors are taking profits, others continue to accumulate Bitcoin.

At least 255 companies are now holding a total of 3.47 million Bitcoin on their balance sheets — around 3.97% of the total supply — up from 124 just weeks ago, according to BitcoinTreasuries.NET.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq indexes rose to new all-time highs on Thursday, fueled by optimism after a better-than-expected US jobs report reinforced investor confidence in the US economy.

“Bitcoin is closely tracking equity performance and stands poised to follow equities to new highs,” Ruslan Lienkha, chief of markets at Cyprus-based crypto fintech firm YouHodler, told Cointelegraph, adding:

“A decisive push above its current consolidation range could see BTC retesting and surpassing its previous all-time high in the near term.”

Still, some analysts predict that Bitcoin’s price will remain capped below $112,000 in the absence of new buyers and the lack of retail interest for the world’s first cryptocurrency, Cointelegraph reported on Thursday.

data.title: Front-quarter set for best ever weekly performance on persisting dry, war weather view
data.description:
The Nordic front-quarter contract was on track for its best ever weekly performance on Friday, as persisting dry and warm weather forecasts for much of continental Europe, including the hydropower-dependent region of Scandinavia, propelled prices.

* The Nordic front-quarter baseload power contract (ENOFBLQc1) rose 0.55 euro to 46.8 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) by 1057 GMT, its highest level since December 30, 2024.

* The contract was on track to log its best weekly performance ever recorded, with rates almost doubling from the previous week, up 99% for the week, as per LSEG data.

* The Nordic front-year contract (ENOFBLYc1) was up 0.15 euro to 35.95 euros per MWh, but was set to log its fourth straight week of losses, down 1.2% for the week.

* "The major models continue to agree on a transition to a high-pressure dominated scenario, as all forecasts show rising pressure over continental and northern Europe in the second half of next week likely lasting until July 20, with no signs of new cooler and wetter spells," LSEG meteorologist Georg Muller said.

* Nordic water reserves available 15 days ahead (PCAEC00) were seen at 7.31 terawatt hours (TWh) above normal, compared with 5.98 TWh above normal on Thursday.

* "The weather forecasts still look quite dry next week, but the hydro balance remains in a surplus and the changed outlook had already been priced the previous day, (so) today, we consider rising prices as the most likely scenario," analysts at Mind Energy said in a note.

* Dutch and British wholesale gas prices were edged up but were trading in a narrow range due to healthy supply and stronger wind output forecasts.

* The Nordic power price for next-day physical delivery (FXSYSAL=NPX), or system price, fell 1.99 euros to 10.8 euros per MWh.

data.title: Corning Inc expected to post earnings of 57 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Corning Inc , is expected to show a rise in quarterly revenue when it reports results on July 29 for the period ending June 30 2025

  • The Corning New York-based company is expected to report a 7.0% increase in revenue to $3.857 billion from $3.6 billion a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 10 analysts, based on LSEG data.The company's guidance on April 29 2025, for the period ended June 30, was for revenue of $3.850 billion.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Corning Inc is for earnings of 57 cents per share. The company's EPS guidance on April 29 2025, for the period ended June 30, was between $0.55 and $0.59

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 11 "strong buy" or "buy," 4 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.2% in the last three months. ​

data.title: GPT Infraprojects Secures INR130 Million Supply Contract from Bangladesh
data.description:
GPT Infraprojects has received a contract worth 130 million Indian rupees from Standard Engineers, Bangladesh, according to a Friday filing.

The order involves manufacturing and supplying mono-block pre-stressed concrete line sleepers for a project in Bangladesh.

data.title: Paolo Sorrentino's 'La Grazia' to open Venice Film Festival
data.description:
Oscar-winning Italian filmmaker Paolo Sorrentino will open this year's Venice Film Festival with his new movie "La Grazia", organisers said on Friday.

Sorrentino, 55, is known for films such as "Il Divo", "The Great Beauty" and "The Hand of God", a deeply personal movie about losing his parents as a teenager, which took the runner-up Grand Jury Prize at the 2021 festival.

"La Grazia" ("Grace"), which Sorrentino also wrote, will screen in competition at this year's event, which kicks off on August 27 and takes place on the Venice Lido, a thin barrier island in the Venetian Lagoon.

It stars his longtime collaborator Toni Servillo and actress Anna Ferzetti.

Little is known about the film. Sorrentino has previously been quoted as saying he and Servillo wanted to make a Francois Truffaut-style love story.

"Paolo Sorrentino’s return in competition comes with a film destined to leave its mark for its great originality and powerful relevance to the present time," the festival's artistic director Alberto Barbera said in a statement.

The Naples-born Sorrentino debuted his first feature film, "One Man Up", in Venice in 2001. He has also previously presented the first episodes of his television series "The Young Pope" at the festival.

"The Great Beauty", about an ageing writer's reflections on life and his search for meaning among Rome's idle rich, won the Oscar for Best Foreign Language Film in 2014. Sorrentino picked up his second Oscar nomination for "The Hand of God".

The 82nd Venice Film Festival will run from August 27 to September 6.

data.title: Canada Monetary Reserves Increased $2.83 Billion in June
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's official international reserves rose by $2.83 billion last month, driven by a net gain on investments and positive reserves management funding operations, the federal finance department said Friday.

As of June 30, the country's reserves of foreign currencies and other monetary assets totaled $127.86 billion, an increase from $125.04 billion the month before.

The government reported no intervention in the foreign-currency market, and there were no gold holdings at the end of the month.

All reserve figures are reported in U.S. dollars.

The finance department said that the amount of Canada bills outstanding rose by $867 million to $2.53 billion as of the end of June. Canada bills are short-term securities sold on the U.S. money market.

At June 30, foreign-currency reserves included securities of $85.84 billion, deposits of $12.69 billion, special drawing rights of $23.27 billion, and a reserve position in the International Monetary Fund of $3.97 billion, the finance department said.

The $2.83 billion net increase in reserves in June involved:

  • reserves management operations up $1.12 billion;
  • return on investments up $716 million;
  • foreign-currency debt charges down $109 million;
  • revaluation effects up $1.10 billion;
  • no net government operations;
  • no official intervention reported.

The currency composition of deposits and securities at end-June included: $67.18 billion U.S. dollars, $15.28 billion in euros, $10.87 billion in pounds, and $5.20 billion in yens.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com
